turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Number 342, Howard L. Nostrand et al., Appellants, versus Thomas Balmer et al. Mr. Hoague, you may continue.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. The Court will remember that, in this case, we are challenging the constitutionality of a Washington state statute which requires, in this case, two professors to take an oath that they do not belong to any subversive organization on pains of being immediately fired in the event they don't take the oath.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The statute, of course, was not restricted to faculty members of the university, is it?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Not of it, no, I was referring in this case only.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: In this case, that's -- is it (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: The statute covers all state, county and city employees.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Throughout the State?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Unless they are exempted by -- throughout the State, unless they are exempted by special act of -- action on the part of the -- a board or agency.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Now, is there not a statutory exemption or regulatory exemption as to non-sensitive employment or some sort of thing?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: There is not?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: So what did you say, Mr. Hoague, about the exemption some of them are not required to do it?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Section 2 of the 1955 Act provides that an agency --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Where that appears?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That appears on page 6 of the appendix to our brief. That an agency may -- well, in some excused certain people, if it is -- if they -- if the agency finds that their -- the employment of such persons will not be dangerous to the health of the citizens and the security of the Government of the United States.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, does that refer to the agency for which a particular person works?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Is employed.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Not -- not a special agency to set up --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Not a special agency.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- or all employees in the state service.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No, Mr. Chief Justice.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: With the Court's leave, it is our intention that I will address the Court particularly on a due process aspects of our argument and Mr. Ringold will deal with the bill of attainder and First Amendment aspects. Of the -- statute under inquiry here, provides that -- I think I'd better read it rather than quote it, that refusal to answer on any grounds shall be cause for immediate termination of such employee's employment. And this, the no provision is for hearing is afforded by the statute. There's no opportunity for the employee to explain his reason for failing to or refusing to sign the oath. Now, we submit that we concede that a State may inquire into the fitness of any employee and into the loyalty of any employee. But that inquiry must accord with fair procedure. There must be a hearing and an opportunity to defend himself or to explain. This requirement was first acknowledged in the Adler case, Adler against the Board, but in that case, the Court found that the Feinberg Act of New York provided an adequate hearing opportunity to explain and in the -- even found that the Feinberg Act only gave rise to a prima facie presumption of disloyalty which would be overcome by any testimony. In the Slochower case, the Court recognized that when a person is -- well, the Slochower case was decide -- appears to have been decided on two issues. One was that the -- the claiming needs -- protection of the Fifth Amendment cannot give rise to a presumption of guilt. And on the further that since there was no hearing afforded the employee that the summary dismissal of him was a denial of due process of law. We submit that that case is -- the reasoning of that case is directly applicable to this Act. Now, of course, the reasoning back of this is that a hearing is necessary to render this firing process or discharge process an inquiry into the fitness of the employee in any real sense, otherwise, the firing procedure bares only a broad sort of dragnet relationship to fitness or security of the employee. It is an -- because there, it is -- well, it is an arbitrary classification of the fit with the unfit and firing of all of them in order to get at the lesser number who may -- who may be unfit or disloyal. And insofar as it is -- is an arbitrary classification, it comes in conflict with the -- this Court's decision in Wieman against Updegraff, the signing -- the refusal of -- to sign the oath by itself bares no reasonable relationship to disloyalty. It is certainly a ground for suspicion of disloyalty. And that would give rise to maybe inquiry as to whether their suspicions were well founded. But in and of itself, the -- it is improper for the legislature to say a refusal to take an oath means that the person shall be immediately discharged. So, the court below --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is this an oath like the Wieman and Updegraff oath?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No, Your Honor, the -- it's -- it will -- it is like, you'd accepted -- I think in Wieman and Updegraff, it implied retroactively, if it --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: The oath appears on page 9 of the appendix to your brief.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, the oath -- oath is on page 9 of the brief. I'm -- I'm -- I -- my memory is that, and it maybe wrong, that in Wieman against Updegraff, it required and just whether they had not belong to a subversive organization. And this only requires that they swear that they do not, at the time they're signing the oath, belong to a subversive organization.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose it is broad as that, it appears subversive organization without defining it?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No it's subversive organization as defined in the Act.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And how is it defined?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: It's defined in the more or less usual very common form, subversive organization, this is -- I'm reading from page 1 of Appendix A, “Subversive organization means an organization which engages in, advocates, abets, advises, teaches or a purpose of which is to engage in, advocate, abet, advise or teach activities intended to overthrow, destroy or alter or to assist in the overthrow, destruction or alteration of any -- of the constitutional form of the United States by,” and I will skip, if I may, “revolution force or violence”.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: There was -- was there a definition in the Wieman and Updegraff as to what is subversive there?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That was a -- I do not advocate that I am a member of any organization that now advocates the overthrow of the Government by force or violence. It describes in the oath what is here described in the statute and not --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Was there also included that belonging to any organization of the Attorney General's list?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, that's a very good opinion.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That is a different thing at this stage of the -- of our -- of our case.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Suppose we -- we -- this Court has held that the so-called “out glory” or the condemnation of the inclusion on the Attorney General's list was in the joint anti-fascist in this case.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But that was too -- too undefined and unlimited.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: But, Your Honor, that was not the basis of the Wieman against Updegraff decision which knocked out the entire oath as applied to any organizations as subversive organization.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's because it -- it had -- it included organizations that might be of innocence as the Republican or Democratic Party. I don't how innocent they are but as much as --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: [Laughs]

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- innocent as they are.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Not -- not if they -- if they were involved in -- add for their purpose to overthrow the Government.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, but the Attorney General's list wasn't restricted to such organization. Besides, I have nothing to bind what the Attorney General could put on his list.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, but this is also --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Oh, that would be a --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: -- it was the --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: So that would (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, that was, that was the scienter and -- and --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I think you had to know that one of these -- I don't know how many years, 100 odd organizations. You had to know that one of these organizations was pursuing the purposes that in your statute is defined.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: And also in the other organization, Your Honor, that says “I'm -- I am a member of any organization that now advocates the overthrow of the Government,” and then further goes on to the Attorney General's list.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes. Well, because -- but that was inclusive. Therefore, for all you know, he might be a member of a -- of an organization that philosophically discussed all sorts of (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: This is correct, this is correct. And certainly, scienter would be more -- more simple as to the Attorney General's list because the list could be obtained at least whereas when it's left in its general form, it puts a very difficult -- in certain cases a, an impossible burden on the person to know whether they have a revolutionary purpose.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible) in the organization to whom -- which he belongs on the list --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: -- and it didn't take that into accounts. I remember I read the case --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That -- that is correct. But that is not the only -- that is not the complete holding. The -- the complete holding applied to more than just the Attorney General's list. It applied to -- it said that there had to a be a knowledge of the revolutionary -- revolutionary purpose of the organization. Now, we submit that the -- well, I -- I will get a little later to the scienter problem. Nevertheless, the reason is -- reasoning of this is that a person might be very innocently a member of an organization. We submit here that a person even knowing of that an organization has a revolutionary purpose, he can be an innocent member. This statute is not confined to active membership or to membership for the purpose -- for the revolutionary purposes. It's very possible for a person to be a member of an organization which is masterminded by revolutionaries which engages in no revolutionary activity actually and devotes itself to other purposes. I -- I just suggest that a person in the State of Washington who was devoted to old age -- to advancing the cost of old age pensions, might very well belong to the Washington Pension Union. There is no other organization out there that works on old -- works to favor old age pensions, and yet, this would have -- have no revolutionary import belonging to this -- this organization which has been declared a subversive organization by the Subversive Activities Control Board.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, this also requires an affirmation that one is not both a subversive person.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: And that comes right back to the same thing, a subversive person is a person who either intends or teaches or advocates the violent overthrow of the Government or is a member of an organization or a member of the Communist Party. So that -- that really doesn't --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: It isn't brought by -- by statutory definition.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: By -- by statutory definition. That doesn't add a great deal. It --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Where did you say the exact form of the oath here?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Right on the last page of our brief, Your Honor, in appendix --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Appendix --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, sir, I have it here. Appendix C, in page 9, Your Honor. Now, the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is that the only one?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: This -- no. This is the one that was presented to the university professors. I have to go out of the record to discuss what the -- the -- state that there are other oaths -- other forms of oaths that were presented to other types of employees but that is not in the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Was it -- was the formerly oath left up to the employing agency in each case or what?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: It was -- I -- I believe it was left up -- actually the oaths were drawn up by the Attorney General and send out. Again, I'm not entirely sure of that, but that -- that's my impression, and that again is not on the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is there any statement any -- anywhere that does require knowledge or the exempts that you do not have knowledge of the purpose?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: The -- yes. On the Communist Party, on -- on subversive organizations other than the Communist Party, knowledge of the subversive purpose is -- is specifically required by statute and that is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Which one is that?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: And that appears in the definition of subversive person.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: On subversive person.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: And that is on page 2 of the appendix to our brief, Section 5. A subversive person means any person, and I will jump to where it says Section -- no. Section -- I'm wrong on this --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Section 3, makes the --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Section 3.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- appellant remain or become a member of one knowing.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, but there is --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is there anything about that in the oath they have to take?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No, no. I am under the impression that the 1953 Act provided as to -- yes. In the 1953 Act, I'm sure, provided as to this oath that as to all -- all subversive organizations other than the Communist Party was not named specifically in -- at that time as a subversive organization. That scienter was required, and I'll read this from the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Thank you. On page 64 of the appellees' brief, starting at the bottom of Section 5, and that's -- yes. This is the 1953 amendment. And it says, “Subversive person means any person who commits,” and so forth, “or who with knowledge that the organization is an organization as described in paragraphs (2) or (3) becomes or remains a member of a subversive organization or foreign,” and so on, “subversive organization”. So that as to all subversive organizations other than the Communist Party scienter is required. As to the Communist Party in 1955, Section 4 says that, “Membership in the Communist Party is a subversive activity.” And this did a way with scienter vis-à-vis the Communist Party.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: This is a definition of a subversive person that you just read. Where is in the -- where is that in the oath that a man has to swear as I still --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: The oath --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- does not belong to one that he knows to be subversive.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Nothing in the oath, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, hasn't your Supreme Court interpreted the oath as including the element of scienter?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: The Supreme Court said that they implied scienter in every portion of the oath and of the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But you --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: They --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But -- but is it -- is it put in the oath that the man is asked to sign?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No, it is not -- it does not read. There's nothing in the -- yes. It -- it does not state knowingly in the oath. It does by reference to the statute.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Because if -- he knows the law and --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: If he knows the law.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- knows that the Court, you said, that he swear and he belong -- he doesn't belong to anything that's knowing it to be so.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: If he knows the law or --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) but does there anything in any oath that shows then that he is doing it?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Does your Supreme -- did you -- has your Supreme Court held that there must be scienter, you're coming to that, you can argue that later.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Well, I'll argue --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: -- it right now, Mr. --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But you -- suit yourself.I just --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- want to know whether your Supreme Court has held that scienter is a requisite.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: The -- the Supreme Court has not held that it's a requisite. They say that it is implied in every part of the oath in the statute. And then they go on to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Does that mean it's implied? Does that mean that there must be -- that if a fellow can say, “I didn't have knowledge,” that would be a good defense and he couldn't be dismissed then? If it's a requisite and if it's implied, legally speaking, it's no different than if it's written there, but what is it means?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: I -- I'm not too sure what it does mean, Mr. Justice Frankfurter. I really not. It -- I think it means that except as to the Communist Party, he cannot be discharged for belonging to a subversive organization unless he knows of the subversive purpose of the organization.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That is required to other statute as you conceive it.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That's not in the oath that he didn't.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Not -- excuse me. No, it is not, Mr. Justice Black. It -- but --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that in a particular case, suppose Professor Jones is now sought to be dismissed under this statute, which you tell me requires scienter, could you attack his discharge by saying that he did not have scienter? I'm not talking about attacking the statute, the -- the particular case of professor Jones, and if the Supreme Court followed its ruling and conversely admits that he didn't have scienter, wouldn't your Supreme Court have to say that they -- he could not be dismissed?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: We will concede that except as to the Communist Party.And as to the Communist --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: As a Communist Party, a fellow knowingly belongs to the Communist Party, that's all you have to show.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That's correct. And --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But he must knowingly belong to that party. He couldn't have been -- he couldn't have -- he -- he could've join as, well, in some (Inaudible) could he?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It seems to me you're conceding more than the oath shows.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Than the oath shows, Your Honor, I am conceding more.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: A man was -- a man was given a paper to sign a note, and he doesn't say anything about that, “I do -- I have not drawn to one knowing it to such --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: This -- this is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- that I'm not belong to any."

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: This is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) some organization it does. But it puts on him the responsibility, doesn't he?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: There's no question about that. That's -- that's correct. I -- we -- we decided to handle this on the assumption that the statute was incorporated into the oath and that everybody is presumed to know the law because we still think that's it's --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I assume we have to handle it each one of us for ourselves, on the basis of whether the oath that he is required to sign is constitutional without that regard that what you think might happen later by your court if they prosecute him on a charge.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. The opinion in the Court --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: A person may know something not merely on the tag that everybody is supposed to know the law, but it maybe such a common -- maybe it's a matter of common knowledge particular by a professor as to what have been declared on this subject by a court. I'm not saying that -- that necessarily follows but it isn't excluded because it isn't written in words and lots of things we know that aren't written in words because, as you say, it's implied. That's why the problem of scienter is so important in this case.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes. Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Now -- now, this case began as an action for declaratory judgment, didn't it?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: These people, of course, have not been discharged. There is an --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: -- injunction against their being discharged.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That's -- that's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: And of course, if -- and -- and these people have, in fact, personally been told and advised by the Supreme Court of Washington that scienter is required in this very case if they know.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: In this very case, except as to the Communist Party.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: And as to the Communist Party, scienter isn't required because the Court says it is common knowledge that the Communist Party is a subversive organization.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, who wrote up this oath?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: If I'm correct, that it's the Attorney General who wrote the oath.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The Attorney General?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: -- former Attorney General.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- I assume that we would have to assume that he also knows what the courts decided.But the oath that he wrote and to which he -- which is on trial before us does not contain any statement that the man has to know that the organization is subversive.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes. The appellees equate and the court below equate their failure to sign the loyalty oath with disloyalty and unfitness, and this is the error that we submit they fall in to, because it is not a true equation, it is only grounds for suspicion. And in the absence of hearings or some sort of inquiry to show that the suspicious are well founded, there is a denial of due process of law. I want to quickly refer to two cases that superficially might be considered applicable but we submit are different. One is the Gerende case, Gerende against the Board of Election Supervisors. In the first place, Gerende was a candidate, was not an office holder and with the thought of the Nelson case as applied to Mr. Globe, the appellant Globe or petitioner, it appears that a person who is an applicant for a job and here a -- a candidate for office is more remote from the property concept, I suppose, of the Due Process Clause. Furthermore, in the Gerende case, the briefs and the court below made no reference to procedural due process. This was not presented to this Court in that -- as far as the briefs are concerned and since --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What case is that?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Gerende. And since it was per curiam opinion, I -- we don't know whether it was considered by the Court. But it -- it at least was not argued. The other is American Communications Association against Douds. And there are number of grounds for distinguishing this case. I think the most important is that the separation from the employment there was indirect rather than direct. A union official who did not take the oath wasn't fired from his job, it merely -- some pressure was put or -- or some benefits were taken away from the union. And this would might normally have the result of his losing his job but the law did not remove him from office.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Are the court decisions in -- are the court decisions in Washington clear that a man who refuses to sign one of these oaths does not get a hearing? I didn't see anything in either brief or in the Court's opinion that -- indicating one way or another whether the statute had been interpreted either to exclude a hearing or to include.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: The statute, of course, says shall because --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: -- immediate dismissal which does not imply a hearing. There is nothing in the opinion of the court below which opinion is most unsatisfactorily in a great many respects and not only -- but -- this respect, but there is -- this is the only holding with regard to this portion of the statute. So there is nothing in -- to show it except this plain wording of it, and the fact that the appellees have not made any such claim. I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Your -- your clients have actually refused to take the oath, the officers on (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, they have that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, have they?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, they have. And they -- they have not --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, did they get notice --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I thought they were asking for a declaratory judgment --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: -- so that the --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: It's all done.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But they have -- they have not refused.Does the record shows they have refused to --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: They -- demand has been made upon them to sign the oath and they were told that they -- they refused that they would be fired. They did not refuse. They brought a declaratory judgement, a petition for declaratory judgment, I was wrong.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Where -- where is the notice that they received in the records? Did it say they would be discharged with -- without hearing or --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: No, Your Honor --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is there anything at all about --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: -- this is in -- as far as the record is concerned, this is in the findings of fact, page 9 of the printed record, paragraph (6) pursuant to the Act, “Defendants have demanded that the plaintiff sign, swear and oath,” and I'm skipping, “I have stated that unless the plaintiff comply with such demands their employment contracts with the university would be terminated by the defendants.”

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Do you say that the Attorney General does not deny that that was performed for the notice given?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: The -- no, but this is defined. This is the basis of --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Oh, we have findings of facts. I see.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: These are the findings of facts --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Oh, yes.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: -- Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: And actually, in the record, if you want to find the exact notice that was sent out, it is in the unprinted record.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Not in the printed record?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Not in the printed record?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Not in the printed record. In the unprinted record, the notice that went out is in the request -- request for admissions of fact, pages 16 to 26 in the original record, and that shows the letter --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The original record is here.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, Your --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: It is the record which --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- is filed with our clerk?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes Your Honor, yes. I -- I state at pages 16 to 27 is --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: And that shows what?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: That shows the letter that went out from the university to the defendants. But we don't -- to -- to the appellants, we don't believe that it is necessary, actually, to do that -- to go to that portion of the unprinted record because the findings of fact which are final, which are not challenged, state that the defendants or there, the appellees here have stated that unless the plaintiffs comply with their demands, their employment contracts with the university would be terminated by the defendants. And that -- that is certainly can't be challenged in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What do you mean it can't be challenged so that means it would be terminated without hearing?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor, I -- I --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: This says, “would be terminated”. You're reading into that would be terminated without hearing, aren't you?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Did you attack it because of lack of a hearing?

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Did your court --

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: This is -- this is the -- this is a -- this is a -- a reason in and out itself why the statute is unconstitutional.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I thought you didn't raise that point below.

turns.name: FRANCIS HOAGUE
turns.text: This is claimed by the appellees. We contradict. We are actually, Your Honor, at a lost to understand it. I would like to wait until he explains it a little more here in -- in reply and rebuttal. Now, with the Court's leave, Mr. Ringold --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: You may. Mr. Ringold.

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Ringold.

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: -- may it please the Court. In the few minutes remaining, I must(Inaudible)my argument considerably. The argument with respect to bill of attainder and with respect to the freedoms under the First Amendment, it had been stated much more eloquently than I could state them by Your Honors in the various cases. I think, however, that there are one or two implications in each of these matters, which I would like to call to the Court's attention. Dealing with the question of the bill of attainder, if this Court will not reexamine the basis of Douds, we must confess the bill of attainder argument goes out the window. We submit, however, that the bill of attainder argument which would dispose of in Douds was disposed of upon an erroneous basis. If we look at the purpose of the proscription of the Communist Party in this particular case, I think it becomes quite apparent that the purpose is to punish that which we cannot otherwise be constitutionally punished. Its purpose is to discourage ideas, to discourage associations. There is no real rational relationship to fitness of the individual who may be a member of the Communist Party. It is an inference drawn that by virtue of the fact that he is a member of the Communist Party, he is a fortiori unfit to teach in the universities. Now, the -- as I read Douds, it seems to say that the oath requirement in Douds would have been considered a bill of attainder except for the fact that it had present and prospective application only. And therefore, it should not be deemed a bill of attainder. We submit that the constitutional provision prohibiting bills of attainder and ex post facto laws being in the disjunctive intended to exclude two separate categories of laws which the founders of the Constitution deem to be improper. There were at least three historic examples of bills of attainder which were prospective in application which were before the constitutional convention, these matters are cited in our brief. And undoubtedly, there must have been a reason for including the bill of attainder along with the ex post facto provision in our Constitution. It -- I think it's significant that there was no discussion at all in the constitutional convention with respect to the acceptance of the prohibition against a bill of attainder. There was some discussion about the inclusion of the prohibition against ex post facto laws. And there was some colloquy where it was said, there is no reason to include ex post facto laws because every lawyer, every civilian knows obviously those are illegal and unlawful. But bills of attainder were not deemed to be unlawful. They were passed by the Georgia legislature, they have been passed by Pennsylvania legislature, and they have been passed by the New York legislature. We found though that there were bills of attainder which were -- which were prospective. And I think that the greatest example of that was the great bill of attainder act was passed by the Dublin parliament under James II. Now, with respect to the First Amendment and the freedoms guaranteed thereunder, it is our position that the language in Adler has been tempered by Your Honors' holding in the Sweezy case. And this, I think, is as it should be. I think that we must consider the necessity for controversy. We must consider the necessity for decent at a university when it is unrelated to the fitness of the individual teacher. The clear and present danger, I assume, that we are talking about which the legislature may -- may legislate against is that of having incompetent teachers. Now, let us assume this example. Let us assume we have a perfectly competent teacher at a university, and he comes to the regions of the university, and he said, “Gentlemen, I have become a neo-fascist. I believe that we should engage in a change in our constitutional form of government so that we have a neo-fascist dictatorship in this country. I am not going to do anything about this in my classroom. I intend to devote, however, my free time outside of the classroom to my political activity in this party.” Now, to me, there is very little difference, very little distinction between that and, for example, the teacher who may be a member of the NAACP. The question must be what relationship -- what danger is there to his views and beliefs being injected into his teaching, into his competency as a teacher.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I thought that our case is regarding whether any -- if he was involved. I think the case involving that we said that their purpose was to enforce of his legal aid to promote the enforcement of a -- of the Constitution to wit the Fourteenth Amendment, isn't that we've held?

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: I believe, Your Honor, it's so held, yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And that's the same, they belong to an organization that seeks to overturn the Government by force and violence?

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: The only -- the only determination, and this, of course, brings us back, Mr. Justice Frankfurter, to the bill of attainder argument. The only determination that has ever been made that the Communist Party seeks to overthrow the Government by force and violence is the determination that has been made by the various legislatures just as an example of that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I can understand that argument, that's a different argument, namely, the -- to characterize the Communist Party as a force or violence party is -- is an intrusion into whatever (Inaudible) person may have because you say that's an arbitrary determination which the legislature can't make, that's your argument --

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- in this point. But that's a very different thing of -- of assuming that what is outlawed is knowing membership in a force or violence party, I just -- I just -- when you (Inaudible) that's just like (Inaudible) I just thought that black isn't white and white isn't black.

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: Except -- should not, Your -- Mr. Justice Frankfurter, should not the guilt be that of the individual not derived at by virtue of his association. If this individual is a member of the Communist Party and he individually subscribes to the overthrow of the Government by force and violence, I have no quarrel, sir. But it must be individual. And it occurs to me, it occurs to --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And in each case, in each case, although the -- although the man is affiliated in a knowing way with an enterprise which seems to overthrow the -- this Government by force and violence as something in our Constitution which prevent a State from saying, “I need to keep such a person employed until I examined each persons whether he understands thoroughly the catechism according to Karl Marx and Lenin, whether he believes in it,” etcetera, etcetera, that's your position, is it?

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: That is my position, Your Honor, and I think that perhaps Your Honor himself stated this in the Douds case when you said, Your Honor, that the cargo article, the faith of our civilization is the inviolate character of the individual, and this, I firmly believe.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Do you think that proves that's -- that bars me from saying that if I voluntarily belong in a -- to an organization, the purposes of which I know and I belong and by my joining add strength to that organization but I must be put on the witness stand and then have a right constitutionally to be -- be searched to what extent I adopt that catechism, is that right?

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: That is my position.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's your position.

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: I'd like to make one more point with respect to that. Your Honor now is assuming that the Communist Party is bad and therefore any membership --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm not assuming anything, I'm putting question. I'm just assuming the question I put.

turns.name: SOLIE M. RINGOLD
turns.text: Very well. Now, if -- is there not a possibility, is there not a possibility that the character, the Communist Party will change and it will revert to the -- to the good old Communist Party of Earl Browder before the Duclos letter as Mr. Browder referred to in the Harper, a month or so ago. Now, if this is the case, and this is conceivable, the statute and the oath proscribe membership in the Communist Party, but is this not a prior restraint upon association because until such time as the legislature has concluded that the Communist Party has changed it's purpose or is now a lawful organization and has become the Browder Party rather than the Foster Party. Until such time, one is taking a risk under our statute and under our oath of associating with that party. Thank you. I'll reserve my few moments left.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Fuller.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court. In this declaratory judgment action, the question before the Court is whether the provisions of Chapter 377, Laws of 1955, State of Washington append the United States Constitution. As counsel has pointed out --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is that -- is that altogether in it or is it as applied here, alleged to be applied here?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Perhaps that could be added, Your Honor. I think the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I -- I'm just wondering, I didn't know.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: The --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is this a broad side attack on the entire acts or is it an attack on the Act as applied here?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, I think the only way to answer that would be to refer to the record and to examine the prayer of the complaint. And in the complaint, the appellants asked for an adjudication, and I'm reading from page 4 of the printed record, “For an adjudication by this Court that Chapter 377, Laws of 1955 violates the Constitution of the United States and the Constitution of the State of Washington and is therefore void and have no effect.” It's on page 4 of the printed record.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you think that that's a surplus to consider it as an attack on the entire act? Did -- was that treated by the Supreme Court in that way or --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: My understanding is that --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- excuse me, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, was it -- was it treated as a charge to the Act as applied to this -- supposed to be applied to this people who violate the Constitution? We don't use to take it up on the whole statute unless we have here. That's what I'm talking about.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I understand that, Your Honor. No, the Supreme Court of the States of Washington considered the Act vis-a-vis the two professors who were involved. I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Mr. Fuller, may I ask you? I've been going through this --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- original record and I can't find that any of these pages that your adversary referred to the copy of any notice to -- either of these petitioners from anybody telling in if he didn't sign, he'd be -- the complaint will be terminated.Was there such notice?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, I must be frank in saying that I do not know. I think that some of the confusion of all parties in this case is due to the nature of the case, the fact that it's a declaratory judgment action.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But didn't --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- they allege that there had been such a notice. Wasn't that the basis so far which they secured the interim restraint against their termination?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: They did so claim.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: They didn't alleged notice, did they? And you said and done -- have demanded that plaintiff's son swore to an oath, than they have (Inaudible) and unless plaintiffs comply with what demands and (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That's -- it was to that that I'm referring --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) if they threatened with termination, they just, I guess, conclusion and they would be terminated --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It is --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: -- if they didn't sign the oath.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Mr. Justice Clark, that's precisely my point because of the nature of the action, because it's a declaratory judgment action and these professors have not actually been dismissed, we can't be certain just what would have happened, whether they would have demanded that they be given a right to rehearing and if they've been turn down, perhaps they could've taken it on appeal to the court below. A part of our case, as you will see as I attempt to develop, is that the fact that in the court below, we claim that the appellants did not raise the question of a lack of any hearing. The reason for the confusion, I believe, is because this issue has sprung full-grown upon the Court on appeal for the first time.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: On appeal where? In the Supreme Court of --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: On appeal to the Supreme Court of the United States.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: They never amended any hearing. Yes, that could be.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I believe I'm correcting in saying that they did not, Mr. Justice Clark.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Are they entitled to any?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I can only answer that we can't be sure. Our Court did not pass upon on that question --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- because it was not squarely before it, Mr. Justice Black

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If -- if they are entitled to any, where does the Act show it?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It is not in black and white. It would be a matter of judicial construction by our highest court but our Court did not have an opportunity to pass upon that because the question was not squarely presented.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Do you have a civil service act that require the hearing before they can be discharged, the general citizens?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: We have no act that would apply to these professors, Mr. Justice Clark.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is there any provision in the -- in any laws, what are they called, bylaws, is there any provision in the matter that govern the intramural life of the University of Washington regarding disciplinary action against nothing of the fact?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: My understanding is, Your Honor, that there are intramural rules that the President and the Board of Regents do follow certain rules as far as dismissing a professor but these rules are not binding upon the Board of Regents.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Are they inviting? Are they -- are they just subtracted or are they formulated by -- in some document?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: On that point, I'm not certain. In any event, it's not binding upon the Board of Regents. Going outside the record --

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Your court didn't pass on the hearing, isn't it, your Supreme Court?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Sorry, Mr. Justice Clark, I didn't hear you.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: And your Supreme Court did not pass on the hearing, did you?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: No, they did not.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Fuller, I notice in the brief that I had here that was filed in the Supreme Court, “On behalf of these petitioners,” set at page 13 of that brief, “who'd be observed under Section 15, Chapter 254, Laws of 1951, the employee may be discharged at reasonable grounds excitably if the person is subversive after notice of charges and hearing. The amendatory language of the Laws of 1955 may confuse when they sign the oath for the answer of the question, grounds for mandatory discharge and denial of a permit without regard to whether the person is in fact subversive and without any notice of charges or hearing.” This is in their brief in the Supreme Court of Washington.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That was on the page 15?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Page 13 of their -- so it is in this record, I believe. So this -- this seems to be that they raise the question that while the 1951 statute originally required a hearing the 1955 amendment repealed that required and now makes discharge mandatory without regard to whether the person is in fact subversive and without any notice of charge or a hearing.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I can only answer --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Is there difference between your 1951 and 1955 statute?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I don't believe that there is. The principle difference between the 1951 statute and the 1955 statute is the fact that in the 1955 statute, the legislature has named the Communist Party as being a subversive organization. I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I refer to Section 15 of your 1951 statute. Have you reproduced that anywhere or is it anywhere in this -- in any of these briefs?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I don't believe that we reproduce that section, Your Honor.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Excuse me --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- we did reproduce it on page 67 of our brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Of your brief? This is the 1951, is it?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I don't know how your facts were found by the judge but in Section 6 of the complaint --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- it was alleged that they had -- and demanded as they sign and swear to an oath, the copy which is Exhibit A unless they comply with the demands and form a contract with the university will be terminated by the defendant. And your answer, you denied this. I'm (Inaudible) The Court found, however, that pursuant to the Act, defendants have demanded that plaintiff sign and swear to an oath. The copy of which is attached to the complaint therein, Exhibit A, and have stated that unless the plaintiffs complied with such demand, their employment contract with the university would be terminated by the defendant. Now, I'd suppose the State of Washington had intended at that time, say that they would not be terminated but they'd wait for a year and somebody would have said it. The courts found that they have given limited term with the notice that unless they signed it, they would be -- employment would be terminated. Are you construing that as being anything less than what it seems to mean on its face?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I will say this, Your Honor, I think that is a lower court decision and the question is what did the -- what did the State Supreme Court pass upon?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, did they overturn this finding?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If so, where?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- I think again we have to reply that we can't be certain because of the nature of the action. Being a declaratory judgment action, the appellants have thrust a number of hypotheticals before the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But this is not -- this is not hypothetical, it was alleged in the complaint. They had been delivered a notice and said if they didn't sign it, they would be fired. You denied in your answer. The District Court found they did, but that was a fact they have done it. Now, unless the Supreme Court has overturned that, are you challenging the accuracy of that finding?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I would say that it was not squarely before the State Supreme Court because the men where not actually fired. They did not actually demand their hearing. They were sitting back --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, that -- but there's nothing said --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- and project them that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- about a hearing. The -- the alligation was that they were told, if they didn't sign this, they -- employment would be terminated and I think you'd want to tie this on the issue of the phrase that the -- it would be terminated, and number 6, if you denied it, the Court found that that was a fact. They'd been told, if they didn't sign, it would be terminated. Now, what reason have you no to say that's not correct? I can -- are you challenging it on the ground that it's based on no evidence? If so, when did you first challenged it?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think, Your Honor, my only answer could be that we feel that that issue was not squarely before the State Supreme Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But -- but why is it not squarely before that? They -- they allege it, and you deny it and the Court find it?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, in our State, the only matter is -- that would come before the Supreme Court would be the matters which have been squarely raised on appeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, if this --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- isn't it squarely raised for year?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: How --how do you determine in your State, how a thing is squarely raised before the Supreme Court? Is there some notice of appeal? Is there some document by which the Supreme Court is appraised of what it is that bare to adjudicate?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, the parts of a case which are on appeal are determined in two ways. Number one, by the notice of the appeal but by ordinary and practice, the notice of appeal was very general. Number two, by specific assignments of error in the brief. Now, in our State, if you do not mean (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is the latter -- is the latter, assignments of error, is that a requirement -- the rule of your Supreme Court?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It is. And if you do not make a specific assignment of error, you're not entitled to raise a point upon appeal.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) specific assignment that there is in this case?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, I do not have --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap)

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- that information specifically before me.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: You mean by -- in the Supreme Court brief?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: They're in the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: They're all here.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: The notice of appeal gives it on page 51 to 53.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Of the record.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's the notice of appeal coming to this Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, you're asking us, as I gather it, the whole that the Court didn't consider this although it's been decided in the court below --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I will say this. In the lower court, we were the ones who did the appeal and they were responding party, so it wouldn't -- the specific points raised on appeal would not be Germane.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: So the -- did you make any assignment of errors?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think we assign --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, did you?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I'm sure that would --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did you make any assignments of errors and where are they? And did you assign as an error the fact that the Court had made a -- the finding was not supported by evidence?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I can only answer that I'm not sure with that, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: So that would -- would that be in the unprinted record that is here? In notice of appeal?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It would be -- it would be in our brief in our notice -- in our notice of appeal, I believe, to the State Supreme Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, I find here you have (Inaudible)2 brief in your Supreme Court but I don't see any brief reviews here in this -- in this unprinted record.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: The thing that puzzles me about this point we're talking about, Mr. Fuller, is every provision of this -- every section of this statute that I read has in it a phrase or clause “shall immediately be discharged”. I mean there's nothing that I find it here about a hearing, “shall, after hearing, be discharged.” This is all --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: No.s

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: -- we show “immediately be discharged”.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think on the hearing point, I will rest my case with what I have said. I can go no further than that if the Court concludes that the appellants are entitled to raise this issue of being denied a fair hearing and dismissal being automatic, I will have to let it go --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: But may I just ask you this, Mr. Fuller?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: May I just -- before you leave that.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Let me say that again. I -- I still don't understand what your position is.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Well, I will say this, that one of arguments was that this Court should not consider the argument of whether a dismissal should be -- of whether the appellants had a right to rehiring before dismissal because the argument was not made before the State Supreme Court. Now, the appellants brief is before this Court. And the Court can look at it as well as I can and I think I will rest my case on that point (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, just let me ask you this.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Are the -- the professors won in your trial court?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: And the State or the agencies of the State took the appeal to your Supreme Court.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, is it your practice in the State of Washington that they may defend their judgment below on any ground available to them whether or not they successfully contest the point you raised on appeal?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. That is the practice in the State of Washington.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, now, didn't they, in this brief, then attempt to defend the judgment below on this -- at the hearing point in that section, that page I read to you?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I don't think they did.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: My reaction is that they did not. No -- I confess that I'm not familiar with that particular page, but I've read the brief a number of times in considering the whole brief. My reaction is that they have not raise a lack of hearing point before the State Supreme Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: They raised -- they raised a hearing point but not this one. Their first point was that no hearing was afforded employee as to character of the listed organization.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Just look at page 13, look at the argument at page 13 that I've read before, that this is this point.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, their argument begins, they say argument in support of judgement.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: At the bottom of page 13.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, I haven't got 13, but I go to the points they make and they begin argument in support of judgments, the Attorney General's list and the first argument under the heading “Argument in support of judgment”. There is no hearing afforded employee as to character of this organization. And they then have 50 more pages, 49 to 50 more pages. And that argument, I do not think, is made and I do not find it in the summary, summary and conclusions on page 85, 86. There are eight points in the parenthesized paragraph form. I do not find that unless oversight skips it.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: In the State of Washington --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: When appellant takes the case, is it his duty to show that the judgment is bad or that the other men's duty affirmative to show that the judgment is good?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: In the State of Washington, an appellant has an affirmative duty to show that a judgment is bad.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: If -- if a case goes up and facts are found, and if appellant wishes to challenge those -- that finding of facts, whose duty is it to do that? The appellant or the appellee?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It would be the appellant, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Whose duty was it to attack the finding 6 if it was bad?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It was the appellant, Your Honor, but --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: As to the State?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is right. The State was the appellant in the lower court. I think that at this point, it might be well to consider this entire statutory system. We're considering three different statutes. And I think part of the confusion comes from that fact. We originally had a 1951 statute which was fairly comprehensive. It was patterned after Maryland's Ober Act. Now, in 1953, an amendment was made to this 1951 statute which changed the definition of subversive person so that only a person who had knowledge of the aims of an organization would be considered as subversive person.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did it also take out of the other act, all the provisions for rehearings?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Which -- which particular provision did Your Honor refer to?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did the -- did the 1953 Act eliminate provisions for rehearing that had previously existed in the other acts?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think that Section 15 which Your Honor refers --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I don't -- I just want to know if the new amendment eliminates the provisions in the prior act which had authorize -- which had required a hearing.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, a short answer to that is no.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: No. All right.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: The 1953 amendment did not do that.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What did it do about hearing?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It had no effect upon any sort of a hearing for the reason as I understand it.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did it take out any part of the language of the prior act that had authorized the hearing?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: None that I know of, Your Honor. It did not affect the 1951 Act in any way as far as the hearing is concerned. It's my understanding. The only thing that the 1953 Amendment did, it's very short, was to bring the 1951 Act into conformity with this Court's decision in the case of Wieman against Updegraff. That's all with the 1953 Act did, as I understand it. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: In what way?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: By changing the definition of a subversive person by requiring that a person could not be defined as a subversive person unless he had actual knowledge of the subversive nature and purposes of an organization.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Was it amendment again after that?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, it was, Your Honor, by the -- the Act which is in question, the 1955 Act. The 1955 Act consists of four sections. Now --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What changes did it make?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I will show, Your Honor. Your Honor, we'll turn to --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: On page 70.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- page 70 of my brief. I think it might be well at this point to read the entire Act, it's very short and it might clear up some questions. Section 1, “Every person, every board, commission, counsel, department, court or other agency of the State of Washington, or any political subdivision thereof who or which appoints or employs or supervises in any matter, the appointment or employment of public employees or employes shall establish by rules, regulations or otherwise, procedures designed to ascertain whether any person is a subversive person.” That would be as defined in the 1951 Act. “In securing any facts necessary to ascertain the information herein required, the applicant shall be required to assign a written statement containing answers to such inquiries as may be material which statement shall contain notice that it is subject to the penalties of perjury.” Now, the new provision, I believe, starts with the word “every”. “Every such person, board, commission, counsel, department, court or rather agency shall require every employee or applicant for employment to state under oath whether or not he or she is,” and I emphasis the word “is”, “is a member of the Communist Party or other subversive organization. In refusal to answer on any grounds shall be cause for immediate termination of such employees, employment or for refusal to accept his or her application for employment.”

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did the prior act contain that provision required immediate termination?

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: That the charge shall be caused by immediate. It didn't say “immediate”.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did it rehab this language to require immediate termination whatever effect it required?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It -- it had the language up to the word “every”. Now, see --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, at -- on page 67 of the -- if that was the earlier one, was it? It said, “He shall immediately be discharged.”

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, that is correct. This --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: That was in relation to the oath, that was in relation to the execution of the statement.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, Section 12, before it was amended, can be found on page 65 of our brief at the bottom of page 65.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I see it. Thank you.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. I think it's not necessary to spend anytime with Section 2 of the 1955 Act because that merely provides that the Act shall not be applicable to positions which are found to be non-sensitive positions.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Could I ask you a question?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Have there been numerous decisions of your court construing this 1950 Act or is this the first one or have there been any decision?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I can think of no decisions, Your Honor. Now, the third section of 1955 Act is not before --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Mr. Fuller, is -- is the Section 2 of the 1955 Act at page 67?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Page 71 of our brief, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: What -- what's this one at, this is your brief, at page 67, what's that Section 2? This section amended by this Section 2. It has the last sentence in it, “Any person failing to execute such a statement shall immediately discharged”? Do you notice that at the end of --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: That was the 1953 Act, doesn't it?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Is that the 1953?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That was the 1951 Act, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: And --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- is that sentence still part of the laws now in effect?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, it is. That particular provision referred to employees who were employed by the State at the time the 1951 Act took effect. First of all, let me be sure, and I'm reading on the same --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: I'm looking at page 67 of your brief.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: At the top of our brief or starting at --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: At the bottom.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- Section 15?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: At the bottom. The last sentence of -- it starts at Section 14 on page 66 --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: -- and it's the last sentence of that section which appears at page 67.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That section is still part of our law, Your Honor. That was not amended by the 1955 Act. It referred to persons who were in government service at the time the 1951 Act took effect.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Now, would that include these propositions?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That I cannot say, Your Honor, that's not in the record. I don't know.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Did they allege they were employed? They attack 1955 Act, is it not?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes. Now, the third section of the 1955 Act is actually not before this Court for its consideration. In that section, the state legislature purported to declare certain organizations which were on the Attorney General's list as subversive organization. This section was declared by our court to be unconstitutional. And the fourth and final section of the 1955 Act added a new provision. It did not amend any previous section except perhaps inferentially the 1951 Act. Section 4 provided, “The Communist Party is a subversive organization within the purview of RCW 9.81,” that's the Ober Act, the Washington's counterpart of the Ober Act, “and membership in the Communist Party is a subversive activity hereunder.” Now, the reason that I've taken this time to point out this system statutes is to show that the 1955 Act is not simply a one shot proposition. It is a part of a pattern of acts noted by our legislature over a period years commencing in 1951. The 1951 Act itself was passed pursuant to executive request in the wake of fact finding hearings by a legislative fact finding committee. Then they amended the Act in 1953 to bring it into conformity with this Court's decision in Wieman against Updegraff. The point is that the legislature passed the 1955 Act not of something completely new but in the light of its experience having worked for the 1951 Act with the previous amendments. At this point, I wish to emphasize one thing. The only thing that this Act prohibits is for a person who is presently a member of the Communist Party or other subversive organization from holding a sensitive position in state government. If a person resigns from the Communist Party today, he can hold a sensitive position in government tomorrow. If a person is a member of the Communist Party today, he can hold the position in state or local government which is declared non-sensitive. Our position is that the Act sets forth only bare minimal requirements.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: On the premise that the Act does not accord a hearing, the one who refuses to sign an affidavit is not a knowing member of the Communist Party. Assuming that's that premise, what's your position as to the constitutionality of the Act in that basis?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, my position is that even if the Act is construed, even assuming arguendo that the Act does not afford a hearing, the Act is constitutional. We think that the Court should first look to determine whether there is a depravation of substantive due process. And if the Court finds there is no depravation of substantive due process, that is that the legislature does have a right to make this requirement, we feel the Court should find that there is no depravation of procedural due process.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Fuller --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I gather these -- I gather that these two men if they -- are sensitive employers because they teach history and romance and language, is that what they teach?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct. One of them teaches romance languages and other teaches history.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) allege that either.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: As far as --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Are those -- are those considered sensitive (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: The way the Act has set up, all positions are considered sensitive unless they are specifically found to be non-sensitive. These positions have not specifically been found to be non-sensitive.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You mean that each might be taken in a special position, professor of -- of history?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, I don't --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I'm asking.

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: Or the football coach?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think that there are positions --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is the football coach a member of the faculty of Washington? I hope not.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I don't believe it is, Your Honor. [Laughter] I think we'll have to just admit right at the start that there are positions which are non-sensitive. But I don't think that the argument is made by the appellants that this position is non-sensitive, the position of a professor in university, professor of romance languages or professor of history.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: That's the case that we have before us. It doesn't differentiate among professors according to the subject matter of their teaching or research, does it?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It does not. The appellants' argument regarding both procedural and substantive due process seems to hinge on five cases. I think that we can approach the heart of the argument if we approach these five cases. The appellants relied upon the Slochower case, the Schware case, the Konigsberg case, the Adler case and the case of Wieman against Updegraff. Now, we submit that these cases can be distinguished that these cases are not authority for the proposition that the 1955 Act is unconstitutional. In the Slochower case, the vice of that case was the fact that the investigation was not made by the school authority but the school authority drew a wrongful inference on the basis of an investigation made by the Federal Government. There's no need to deliver this point here because this point was made by the Court itself in the Beilan case and case of Globe against Los Angeles. Now, in the Adler case, the appellants argue that because an administrative hearing was necessary in the Adler case, it would be a depravation of procedural due process not to afford a hearing in the present case. And here, we come to one distinction which is perhaps the greatest distinction in the entire line of cases. New York's Feinberg Law referred not only to present membership in an organization but the Fienberg Law said that a person could be dismissed for having committed the act of joining a subversive organization. Here, we are concerned only with present membership. Now, it's understandable that if a person can be dismissed for mere past membership, it might well be a constitutional requirement that this person be afforded a hearing. But in the present case, where it is only present membership, it is not necessarily to inquire into the reasons why he resigned from the party because he didn't resign, reasons why he joined, the Court was interested in seeing that a person did not wrongfully suffer. If a person is knowingly, presently a member of a subversive organization, we submit they could not be held to be innocent. Now, one of the most important cases bearing upon this case is the case of Wieman against Updegraff. Mr. Justice Frankfurter asked a question earlier, he said, “Was the oath in the Wieman case similar to the oath in the present case?” The answer to that is no. The oath in the Wieman case was different because it referred not only to present membership in a subversive organization but it also went back, I believe, five years.
