In other words, in the case of Wieman against Updegraff, if a young person were to join an organization, maybe even the Communist Party, were to learn of its true aims and were to resign from the party within a week, that fact which stand as a bar to his employment. In this case, there is no such impediment. Now, because the Oklahoma Act would bar a person from employment who innocently joined an organization which was actually subversive, this Court held that it violated -- it was a deprivation without due process. In the present case, it is difficult to see how a person could say that he is innocent, either he knows of the -- the purposes of an organization or he does not. If he does not know the purposes of an organization, he would automatically sign the oath.And it's difficult to see how he could be held guilty of perjury.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Why?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, I said that if a person --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I understand that by why? He -- this doesn't say that he will not belong to oath that they gave him, it doesn't say that he will not belong to an organization which he knows to be subversive that he does not. It says he does not belong to one.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct. And that has been construed by our court, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: When -- when has it been held by your court that a man is not responsible for -- for it on the basis you suggest? I'm talking about a holding of the Court.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: There has been none because --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: There has been none.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- this is a declaratory judgment action and that is part of the difficulty. We are dealing with hypothetical situations. There is no clear case before the lower court. This Court is -- we have to guess to what a situation maybe in the future. The court has held (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I suppose -- I suppose the man has to sign his oath as against whether he -- what will happen to him if he belongs to one if they may later turn out on the evidence to be subversive within the meaning of the statute.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Mr. Justice Black, getting down to cases, the oath says, “That I am not a subversive person or a member of the Communist Party or another subversive organization for another or otherwise, which engages in or advocates, abets, advises,” and then it goes from there. “But the point is I am not a member of any subversive organization or a member of the Communist Party.” If a person does not know that an organization is subversive and our court having held that scienter is implied in every part of the oath.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But when did it hold that in the case? Which case is --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It's --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- that that it holds?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- it's in the decision, it's in the record. It's -- it was in this case, the present case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You said that was a holding.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: In this declaratory judgment.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: That was a holding that these particular people had to sign this oath and they would know that they would not be held liable if they were innocent of the purposes of the organization.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: The Court held that scienter was implied in each and every part of the oath. Now, of course, as I've said before this is a declaratory judgment action that's part of the difficulty. The specific facts are not before this Court. In a declaratory judgment action, the lower court held that scienter was implied in every part of this oath. We submit that the difference between past membership and present membership is decisive. A person might well be inveigled into an organization innocently. But it is different if he is presently a member of a subversive organization or of the Communist Party.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Could one, in your view, under the statute, resign today and then make this affidavit the next day, tomorrow?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. That is one of our most important points. A person who is a member of the Communist Party or any other subversive organization could resign today and take the oath tomorrow.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, might he not fall then under the -- under the portion of the Act which says, “I am not a subversive person”? Is there question of whether he actually belongs or not determine whether he is subversive person?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, as opposing counsel has said this again comes back to the question of whether he belongs to a subversive organization because the definition of a subversive person in our statute is one who belongs to a subversive organization. So if he resigned from the subversive organization today, he would not be a subversive person tomorrow.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Oh, am I wrong that your -- your statute provides that whether a person belongs to an organization or not if he -- if he does advocate force and violence he's a subversive person? I thought --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: No that --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- I would just read this very hastily, and I don't know whether -- whether I read it correctly or not, but I thought they made that distinction that if -- that if an individual did advocate force and violence that he was a subversive person and that without regard to whether he belong to an organization or not because your Act says, I am not a subversive person or I do not belong to the Communist Party or any other organization. And then there is a definition that I read some place in -- in here of what a subversive person -- what is a definition of a subversive person. I thought --

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) brief, Mr. Fuller.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Now, here it is, I -- I think on page 63 of the -- or 64 of the appendix to your brief. This is the statute, it says, self -- says -- over on page 64 in the second full paragraph, “Subversive person means any person who commits, attempts to commit or aids in the commission or advocates, abets, advises or teaches by any means, any person to commit attempt to commit or aid in the commission of any act intended to overthrow, destroy or alter the -- the Government or who is a member of a subversive organization or a foreign subversive organization.” Now, suppose if he belong to -- to the Communist Party and had scienter and then resigned as you say one day and took the oath, wouldn't he be guilty of -- wouldn't he be guilty of perjury if he -- if he actually fell under this definition?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct. Your Honor has asked me -- asked me originally the question, “Could a person resign today and be considered a non-subversive person tomorrow?” My answer was yes, based upon the organizational membership but that would have to be qualified by the section which Your Honor has just read --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- regarding individual advocacy.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I was referring to only the portion of the Act --

turns.name: JUSTICE DOUGLAS
turns.text: He might -- he might require (Inaudible) to make sure he was really non-subversive.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Sorry, Your Honor, I can't answer that.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Of course, the other section (Inaudible)

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, it is. We submit that the legislature has been reasonable in passing such an act. It's true the -- the Court must balance the effect upon the individual with the societal interest to do that in every case. But here, the legislature has said that they do not want people who are presently members of the Communist Party or other subversive organization to hold sensitive positions in our government. Now, there's no need to dwell upon the communist danger. I realized that point can be over dramatized. I think this Court, itself, in the Adler case pointed out that there was a danger of communist propaganda to settle, to allow detection being disseminated in our classrooms. This is the danger which the legislature was seeking to prevent. And we submit that on the basis of the case law of this Court, the legislature had a right to do so. There are two cases which are almost exactly in point both of them were referred to by counsel. The first case is the case of the American Communications Association against Douds. The second case is the Gerende against Board of Election case. Now, we submit that the distinction made by counsel in the case of American Communications Association against Douds is a distinction without a meaning. In the Douds case, this Court held that Congress had a right to have a non-communist affidavit and to demand that union leaders, who are members of union seeking to avail themselves in the provisions of the National Labor Relations Act, signed this non -- this non-communist affidavit. This is very similar to the present case. In that case, the Court held that the danger of political strikes was sufficient to justify this abridgement of the right of the individual. The Court held that the action that Congress had taken was reasonably related to the danger involved. We submit that is true in the present case. Now, the distinction that counsel pointed to by saying that it affected the individual only indirectly by providing that the labor union could not avail itself of the National Labor Relations Act is a distinction ought to mean because it had the effect of discouraging Communist Party membership. It had the effect of affecting future action the same as the requirement -- the affidavit requirement in the present case. Now, it's true, the -- in the Gerende case, there was only a per curiam decision. But on the other hand, the plain holding of that case is, the plain effect to that case is a that a person who is a member of the Communist Party was not able to sign the oath and to be elected to office in the guise of belonging to one of the major established parties. There again, the Court inferentially at least held that the danger of this happening was sufficiently great to force a man to come forward and state his views. Because of the limited time remaining, I will not spend much time on the bill of attainder argument especially since counsel concedes that under the law, this Court should hold that there is no bill of attainder in the present case. He is asking that the Court overturn its holding in the Garner case and in the Douds case. Our point would be this. Since there is no penalty involved, since there is no penalty involved in the Douds case, there is no penalty involved in the present case. And if there is no penalty involved in the present case, it would constitute a mere advisory opinion for this Court to state that a bill of attainder could be prospective in operation as well as being retrospective in operation.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What was the penalty in that case, the Lovett case?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: The penalty in the Lovett case was a previous act committed by Mr. Lovett and Mr. Douds and the other individual involved.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: The penalty?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: There is nothing --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What was --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: There was a penalty.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: What penalty?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: The penalty was a writer attached to an appropriation act which had the effect of dismissing them from their jobs for past activities. We submit that there's no penalty involved in this case and therefore, the Court should not pass upon the bare question of whether there --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If -- the -- in the loose sense of the term there's a penalty but what you're saying is that it isn't for past conduct.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Mr. Justice Frankfurter, I think that --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Is there penalty in -- in the loose way in which this is used, namely, something that hurts?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: In the loose sense, it's something that hurts. Now, in the Lovett case, you, yourself, Mr. Justice Frankfurter --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, but that was an individual opinion.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I understand that.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) the whole concept of bill of attainder to question that opinion.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I understand that --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- Your Honor but I think that the language is particularly --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I suppose -- I suppose the language would have provided that you lose your job, that it'd just be loose language but (Inaudible) you'd lose your job.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor, but you have to measure the interest which is being protected. If a person loses his job as a result of the Government trying to protect students in the schools from being a part of a captive audience where they would be subjected to communist propaganda, if a person inferentially loses his job, we submit that that would not be such sufficient penalty to constitute a bill of attainder.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, is it your point really that -- that this is post facto which tells you either consequence is not something in the past but something in -- in the present, therefore in the future?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct. I think that as you, yourself, said --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: You seem to find more comfort from what I've said than I got from anybody else in saying [Laughter].

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think, Your Honor, said in the Lovett case, in an individual opinion, that if it is not an ex post facto law, the reasons it is not are persuasive that it cannot be a bill of attainder.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But you see, we frequently hear that the dissent is not the opinion and the -- the majority (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So that's why I've been trying to stop you.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I am not --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I've been trying to stop you.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I am not citing this as being authority.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: (Voice Overlap) it wasn't the dissent.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I'm not citing this is case authority.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: As I recall it, that was considered.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: But I feel that the language is apt in this case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: May I ask you about the Gerende case. Of -- one of the sentences in this case was this, and I ask what you think it meant. We read this decision the whole that to obtain a place on Maryland ballot, a candidate need only make oath that he is not a person who is engaged in, in one way or another, in the attempt to overthrow the Government by force or violence, and that he is not knowing a member of an organization engaged in such attempt. What do you understand that in engaged in?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I would understand that to mean the present tense. That he's actually doing?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Mr. Fuller --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: So do you think that's (Inaudible) from (e) of your Act?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Section (e) of our Act?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes. They're on page 64 --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Yes, page 64.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: -- of your brief.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Our Act also puts things in the present tense, if that is Your Honors' point. It should be notice that the language in the bottom of Section (e) on page 64 the language in brackets is no longer a part of that section. That has been amended by the 1953 amendment which was made in the wake of the case of Wieman against Updegraff.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, your (e) goes a little further than that. It may not be important, but the Court read that as only putting the penalty on actually engaging in or belonging to an organization that was actually engaging in an attempt to overthrow the Government by force or violence. I'm just saying that --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your -- your point --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- because I don't (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- is that they're not identical.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- to be read as meaning more than and I think it mean I agree to it and that -- that sentence is very important in considering that particular case. I do not mean to say if you do not have other cases that would support your argument.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I understand that, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: If you will read, that shall mean that a man must throw a bomb?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Do I read subsection (e)?

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: No, the Gerende case. Just the sentence that Justice Black read, does that mean the man must throw a bomb --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It might be (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: -- to be engaged in?

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- axe or gun.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Or he might write a -- might write a powerful pamphlet. We've heard again and again with a pamphlet, that idea is weapons.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Does this (Voice Overlap) --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That would be my personal reaction.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- does this opinion indicate that would base on any extent on Earth on that -- that idea?Do you get --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- that from it?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- it is the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Do you get from the idea that a man actually engaged in doing something, engaging in an effort at the moment to overthrow the Government, does that refer to speech or writings?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I can only answer that I do not know.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, but we held we've had cases in this Court in which people have been sent to jail for long years for spreading documents apart -- among soldiers or officers and thereby, obstructing the war effort. You have to throw bombs in order to be mischievous.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: I -- I stated to you that there were cases that might support it, but this one is limited to actually engaging in the conduct not the speaking or the writing.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: And I'm asking you whether -- whether disseminating literature is engaged in something.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: My reaction would be that it would be. Now, Mr. Justice Black --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, I would say it's --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: -- I would say it's engaged in something it's in free speech and free press, yes.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I've given my reaction on it and I can do no more, Your Honor. Turning just for one moment to the claim, the third argument of the appellants that the 1955 Act constitutes a violation of the First Amendment. I think we will first point to the case upon which the appellants rely and then attempt to show that some of the cases which support our position. Now, the principal case which was cited was the case of West Virginia State Board of Education against Barnette. We submit that this case can be distinguished from the present case because in the case of West Virginia State Board Education against Barnette, this Court held that the Government could not force a man to profess something in which he did not believe. In the present case, we are forcing a man to profess as to what he does belief. Now, there is a difference in the interest of the government to be protected because in the West Virginia case, the Government could show no interest in forcing to a man to go through a ritual to say something which he did not believe. But here, the interest in protecting the children and the students in the schools is sufficient, we submit, to warrant the restriction and the rights of an individual to force him to come forward and make a positive statement. We submit that there is an entire line of cases which holds that the 1955 Act is valid. We submit that Douds case is authority for that proposition. In addition, there's the case of United Public Workers against Mitchell involving the Hatch Act. In that case, this Court held that the interest of the Government in maintaining a loyal civil service was sufficient to warrant the abridgement of the rights of those civil servants to the extent that they could not engage and I believe the words were actual -- active political management. Again, in the Garner case, the Court held that the City of Los Angeles had a right to force a man to come forward and make statement that the interest of the State in maintaining loyal employees was sufficient to justify that. Now, finally, and this is the final case that I wish to mention to the Court. There is the recent case of Speiser against Randall. And at first blush, it maybe surprising that I would mention this case. It will be noted that it is not cited in the appellant's brief. Even though the courts stuck down an oath this case and to my knowledge this and the case of Wieman against Updegraff are the only recent cases were an oath has been struck down. Even though an oath was struck down in this case, there is still language in that case to the effect that where the requirement more limited, had the requirement applied to a more specific group such as perhaps school teachers or such as perhaps union leaders as in the -- the Douds case require the -- the Act may have been saved. So the Court not only made the distinction by, in our opinion, reaffirm these previous acts. In conclusion, let me say that it's always difficult for a lawyer to ask for an abridgement to any sort of human liberty, to ask for restriction of that liberty. But there are times when a restriction is necessary as is shown by the opinions of this Court, and we submit that this is one of those times. Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Well, the opinions of this Court don't show that that which may have been sustained but necessary or even desirable. All it shows it didn't transgress constitutional limits.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: This Court is (Inaudible) in affirmative judgment on the wisdom or desirability or need of any legislation that makes a State.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor. This Court does not make any judgment as to the wisdom of the legislation and that is why arguments --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: Or as to its need.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Or as to its need, that is correct. The only thing that this Court will consider --

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: I may think (Inaudible) legislation one does think (Inaudible) legislation that doesn't seem to -- it doesn't offend the limits of constitutional authority of States (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: We feel that is correct, Your Honor. Thank you.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: May I ask you please, sir?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Justice Whittaker.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Section 15 shown at page 67 of your brief still in -- in the law, of your law? Starting out reasonable grounds on all the evidence to believe that any person is a subversive person as defined in the Act shall be caused for discharge. And it says, “The Attorney General and the Personnel Director etcetera shall, by appropriate rules, prescribe etcetera regulations and shall have the right on reasonable notice to a hearing after deferred by himself and witnesses.” Now, are those rights accorded under the statute as it stands today?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: My answer would be, Your Honor, that the 19 -- the Section 15 was not affected by either of the 19 --

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: I don't understand you. Does not what?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Was not affected, not affected by either the 1953 or the 1955 Act and it would still be good today.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, then, what is your --

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Except -- I'm sorry, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Except as to membership in the Communist Party.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: 1955 Act proscribed that.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Then, do you understand this statute in terms to require a formal hearing?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That's why we seem to provide, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, why didn't you argue that in the beginning if that's the case?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Because that point was not directly argued.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: It's been argued -- it's been argued here back and forth or (Inaudible) Are you saying now that your law gives these people a chance to adhere in this section they cannot be immediately discharged as the Court found but because it would violate this section?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I'm saying that might well be one interpretation of it. But my argument was even assuming arguendo --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Well, has -- has it been so interpreted with reference to college professors or had you ever argued it in this case up to this moment?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: No, it has not been so interpreted because --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Had -- had you ever argued it in this case up to this moment?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct, I have not, but there has been no case coming before the Court under a specific set of facts.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, may I interrupt to ask you there? Has the Washington Supreme Court held that these petitioners may be forth with discharge without a hearing in this case?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I have to ask a question before I can answer that, Mr. Justice Whittaker. For a membership in the Communist Party or for a membership in another organization?

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, for failing -- let me just say it this way. Has the Supreme Court of Washington held that these petitioners may be forth with discharge for failure to make this -- assign this form of affidavit submitted?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think the only answer would be that the Court has not pass squarely upon it. The Court held that every part of the oath should contain scienter. The -- the Court -- the Court did not make a specific holding regarding right to a hearing.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: But it did say to reverse judgment, isn't it?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct. It did reverse the judgement.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: And it did so on -- despite the fact that the there was a finding they had been told, they'd be (Inaudible)

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: That is correct, but we submit that in their briefs, the appellants here, the respondents below did not argue the point of fair hearing. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: But, Mr. Fuller, on page 68, as -- as Justice Whittaker read to you, the Attorney General, the Personnel Director and the Civil Service Commission of any county and so forth shall, by appropriate rules or regulations, prescribe that persons with -- being subversive and so forth, have -- has your office ever prescribe any such rules?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Your Honor, I know of no such rules.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Has the Personnel Director of the State ever done so?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I know of none.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Has -- has anybody who is ever been discharged in your State been accorded any such hearing?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: As to that, Your Honor, I would have to say that I do not know.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: Do you know if anybody ever has been discharged?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: No I do not know of anybody who has actually been discharged.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: Of course, if the --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Would -- would you say that there had not been any discharge?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I would say I believe that to be the case, Your Honor. I'm hesitant to be causative --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, I -- yes, yes.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: -- but I know of no case where anybody has been discharged for that reason.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Do you know of any proceedings in which someone has actually sought a hearing?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: No, I know of none.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: I thought your contention was that the 1951 Act provides for a hearing. It was still in effect and that the 1955 Act would afford a hearing. The 1955 Act did not amend the -- I mean did not repeal the hearing provisions of the 1951 Act.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: We feel that it did not.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: How could you give these people a hearing? How could do they have had a hearing if you didn't have any regulations as the Act proscribes?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I'm sorry. I didn't understand that. How could you give them a hearing?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Well, you -- the question we raise, whether they asked a hearing, you say they -- you think they could -- they should be given, they have to be given that hearing. How can you give them a hearing if you don't -- if you never have any rules or regulations under this Act under which they could be heard?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, of course, you're correct, if something came up, they would be necessary to start from the ground up and to have actual regulations and to -- to hold a hearing. But it couldn't be done without the rules and regulations which you mentioned.

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Fuller, Section 15 deals with a person charged with being a subversive person. There's nothing to do, does it, with the person who refuses to take -- sign the oath? Aren't they two very different things?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think that is correct. But again, I think ---

turns.name: JUSTICE STEWART
turns.text: So that -- so that the hearing provisions of Section 15 really don't help us in the -- on the question whether a hearing will be given to one who fails or refuses to sign an oath, isn't that so?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: It seems to me that there is some uncertainty here, Your Honor, because the Court has never passed upon this, we do not know. Now, what you say sounds correct, I can only answer that our court has not passed upon that.

turns.name: JUSTICE CLARK
turns.text: But the Attorney General or you think that the -- would be entitled to a hearing, I believe, you said a few minutes ago?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think that might well be the case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did you say then?

turns.name: JUSTICE BRENNAN
turns.text: Are you --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Did you say that in your (Inaudible) representing the Attorney General of the State of Washington, that you believe that these people are entitled to a hearing under any statute that now exist if they do not sign this, and if so, where is that statute?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I'll make the statement for the Court on that very point. Representing the Attorney General, it is my opinion that under Section 15, it has been referred to the individuals affected might well be entitled to a hearing.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Might well be.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Might well be. Now, I can't be certain because I'm not sure how our court would -- I can't guess what our court would say but I was --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You -- you are guessing.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: In a sense that is it. My interpretation would be, they might well be entitled to a hearing.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: As to whether or not they are a subversive person.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Not as to whether they are members of the Communist Party.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: No. Now, I should make that provision there. I -- I will say this, that as far as membership in the Communist Party is concern --

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: You mean if they refused to sign that oath, they can get a hearing still on the ground that they are not a subversive. Is that what you mean?

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I'll say this. That might well be the case. I can't be certain.

turns.name: JUSTICE BLACK
turns.text: Might -- might well be.

turns.name: JUSTICE WHITTAKER
turns.text: Well, isn't that the statute says? Does it mean this thing, I don't know, that's the (Inaudible) That is Section (Inaudible)

turns.name: JUSTICE FRANKFURTER
turns.text: But we're all guessing as to what the Washington Supreme Court will say what its statute means. That's necessary the guess by everybody on this bench or off the bench.

turns.name: HERBERT H. FULLER
turns.text: I think that is correct, Your Honor. My personal interpretation is that it would afford them a hearing. That is my personal guess as to what the Supreme Court would do.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE WARREN
turns.text: Thank you.
